Question: What do I like about Celebrations with my family?Language Arts related to the Unit topic: Question for Week

One: What are some festivals or celebrations celebrated with my family?NOTE: If there are Jehovah Witness children in the class, the teacher will need to modify this ILS. The teacher must not assume that every child celebrates their birthday.Role Play/Video followed by DiscussionThe teacher will organize and celebrate a surprise birthday party with the class (pretend). The classroom will be set up for a Birthday Party with real party hats, music, decorations, food etc. The teacher can solicit the assistance and perhaps

some contributions from the parents without the children's knowledge. Perhaps the party could be for a special stuffed toy that is in the classroom, or a mascot. After the party, the teacher can discuss with the children. What did we celebrate today? What is a celebration? Do you celebrate your birthday in some way? What happens on your birthday?The teacher may show a video or read a book or tell a story about a surprise birthday party, and then have a discussion with the children. Suggested Links:Clifford's Birthday Party
VideoHappy Birthday Cupcake

Read Aloud BookDiscussion after the book:What did you see in the book? Do you have some of the same ways to celebrate your birthday? What was different in the book we read/saw?Before, during and after the story, the teacher may incorporate some mini-lessons. For example, the teacher may help the children to identify and reinforce the /u/ sound using words from the story

Happy Birthday Cupcake. (cupcake, muffin, sun, lunch, just, but, much, crumble, ketchup, surprise). Learners can listen for the sound in words, identify the location/position of the sound in words and discriminate sound from other sounds.(Please refer to the Curriculum Outcomes and Guide for Effective Strategies for further ideas to incorporate skill development alongside a read-aloud).Further Discussion:What other festivals or special events do you celebrate in

Learning CentresThe teacher will review the idea of national and religious celebrations during a large group discussion.The teacher will set up the classroom with various centres depicting different celebrations (religious and national) with materials and labels (E.g. Christmas Corner
labels: Christmas tree, lights, foods etc.). The class is divided into small groups of 4-6 and learners are given the opportunity to visit the different centres and explore the displays
manipulating materials, sharing thoughts and ideas,

exploring materials, trying on costumes/garments etc. The teacher moves around to each centre and encourages conversation by asking questions as learners interact with the materials and discuss with peers. The teacher may also use various materials (particularly unfamiliar items) to demonstrate their useOREach small group will be assigned a specific centre where the learners will explore the display/materials, discuss with their peers guided by the teacher. Each group will then share with the whole class on the celebration that their group explored.After the exploration at the learning centres, the teacher may hold a discussion with the full group:What celebrations did you learn about? What can you tell about this celebration? Where did these celebrations take place? Who took part in the celebrations? Can you name some things/items that are used in this celebration? Why do we have these celebrations? Which of these do you celebrate? Which is your favourite celebration? Why? For example: how many learners celebrate birthdays with their family? How many learners like carnival? (Learners count and teachers record number of learners., making a list on chart paper or on the board.)

The teacher will encourage the children to count along with her/him. The teacher will point out the numerals and have children repeat.Information/data collected can be placed in a simple pictograph which learners will learn about collecting data, representing data using pictorial representation and also read the data presented. This question about favourite
celebrations is a good example to be used for this purpose.Sorting and Classifying
Data: CountingWho likes celebrating Christmas with their family? Let's count and shade the number of boxes.
Language Arts and Social Studies -Resource Person and Thank You Note

Note: Before the Resource person comes, the teacher will have a discussion with the children about questions they might have for the visitor. The Resource person may be an adult who works in the school, a parent, a community person.Presentation by Resource PersonInvite a parent/resource person to come and set up the classroom for an actual celebration that is different from the ones commonly known to children such as Diwali or a celebration that persons of other races such as Chinese celebrate. The teacher will help the children realize that

celebration can be national or religious. Children identify the type of celebration.During the visit, the teacher will encourage the children to ask questions, and the teacher will support the Resource person who is presenting to young children.Discussion Questions (after the presentation)What did you see? What are they used for? What did we learn about celebrations today? Why do we have this celebration? Is this a national or religious celebration? Why? What are some things used during this celebration? What other festivals do you celebrate in your family?Children will respond to the presentation by completing a response sheet, with the teacher's assistance.

Studies: Picture Sort GameA set of ten (10) pictures that are related to different celebrations will be placed in two boxes (one for each team). Two sets of containers will be labelled with the words "Religious" and "National". Learners will be divided into two teams. They will then compete to pull the pictures from the boxes and place them in the appropriate containers. For example, a Christmas tree placed in the container labelled "Religious", a flag would be placed in the container labelled "National". After some time, the children count the number of pictures in each box. They could compare to see if each team sorted the pictures in the same way.

Introduction to Week three Field Trip or Celebration Display The teacher and learners may visit a family home (pre-arranged) to observe a family celebration such as Diwali or the teacher may invite a resource person (parent) to the school to showcase a family celebration. They could set up the classroom area as if hosting the real event and give learners the experience of that family celebration. Interaction/discussion Learners are encouraged to ask questions to learn the names of various decorations, food/dishes, clothing, musical instruments and other items that may be unfamiliar. Learners are exposed to new vocabulary to describe the way things look and taste. What is the name of this dish? What is it made with? What is the reason for having these decorations? Who did you learn this celebration from? Is this a religious celebration or a national celebration? Group Work

Picture Collage Learners will work in small groups to create a picture collage depicting the family celebration that they had experience of during the field trip or class display. Learners are given picture cut-outs from online or old magazines which they select and paste on construction paper. Or, the children may draw some of the items they remember. The more advanced learners can categorize the pictures as foods and decorations while the early levels can make one big collage. Learners are encouraged to label items and write simple sentences about their creation, with the teacher support when necessary.

inclusive_learning_strategies: Language Art and Social Studies - Read Aloud about a Wedding CelebrationBook

Title: Maisy goes to a Wedding by Lucy Cousinshttps://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8ICAcllAoD4The teacher will display the cover of the book, identify and read the name of the title, author and illustrator. Briefly have the children talk about the cover of the book and what they think

will happen in the story. The teacher will make note of the children's responses. Invite children to talk about how they celebrate weddings with their families. Invite children to talk about foods, decorations and events such as the reception or party. The teacher points out that good listeners help themselves understand the story by thinking about what happens in the story and try to guess what will happen next. They also ask questions about things that happen in the story

Does that make sense? Read the story, pausing at strategic points for children to predict what will happen next (children can also draw their predications) and to check their predictions with what actually happened in the story. Pg. 2

What do you think Maisy will wear? The teacher will model think aloud to help the children to practice similar habits. I am trying to guess what Maisy will wear. It is a wedding and people usually wear fancy/ pretty clothes. I think she might wear a pretty dress with flowers and frills.

inclusive_learning_strategies: Responding after Reading After reading the teacher asks this question: How is this wedding similar or different to one you attended? What is the same? What is different? Responses are noted on chalk board. (Please see the Curriculum Outcomes and Guide for Effective Strategies for suggestions about Shared Writing.) In groups of two's and three's, children will talk about a wedding - how they might plan a wedding for a family member. What special foods would you make? How will you decorate the church or places? What should happen at the wedding? Each small group will share what was discussed with the large group. The teacher will help the children to understand that some ways to celebrate are the same, but also some ways are different.

Then, in large group, the teacher will point out to the learners that we can remember what happened in a story by looking at the pictures or illustrations. Children and teacher browse through the book (without audio track) to observe the pictures and talk about what they see. The teacher will show the children the story one more time and ask them to focus their attention on the decorations noting the different shapes and patterns. The teacher can show the children the triangle flags and identify the patterns made with the decorations. The teacher and learners discuss what a pattern means and the importance of patterns. The teacher and learners

explore ABAB patterns using materials in the classroom. The teacher may demonstrate how to make an ABAB pattern with two colours. Product Children are given materials - different coloured triangles, paper strips, glue and strings. The learners will use the triangles to create 2-colour patterns. The teacher will help the children to attach the triangles to the string, and then put them up to decorate the classroom.

Language Arts and Social Studies - Important jobs During a Celebration The learners had been asked to come to school with 2-3 items or pictures of items that they have used for a family celebration recently. If some learners are waiting to show and tell about the foods, decorations and events of the specific celebrations, this ILS could start with some sharing. The teacher will let the children know that they will be listening to another story about a wedding. The teacher could ask the

children if they know what a "ring bearer" is and if any of them had ever done that job during a wedding."The Ring Bearer"https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=SgjmpdUIQhQ(Please see the Curriculum Outcomes and Guide for Effective Strategies for steps for the teacher to use during a Read Aloud).After the reading, the teacher will print this sentence on a chart or the board:"Mama's having a wedding and Jackson has an important job to do."Printing the sentence with the children's help is a Shared Reading/Shared Writing activity. (Please see the Curriculum Outcomes and Guide for Effective Strategies for ways to enhance the literacy skills during this time.)Ask the children what was one important job that Jackson had to do.The book may be shown again, and children will listen to what Jackson has to do. The teacher may print a sentence, or make a list of what Jackson has to do. The class may discuss all the preparations
foods, decorations, clothing
that are part of a wedding celebration.Story Quilt

Children will design a four-block story quilt with paper. The squares can depict four scenes in sequence of the story. The pictures will include the food, decorations, jobs, clothing that were part of the story. The teacher may print the words: food, decorations, jobs, clothing on the board, and the children may label their drawings (as they are able).

Interactive Writing: Sharing the PenAll about Diwali
The teacher will re-read book/story from week two. The children will tell what they remember about the Diwali festival. The teacher will ask the children to consider

Decorations and FoodLearning CentresIn small groups, the learners will revisit the various learning centres established in week one and interact with the items associated with that celebration

decorations, foods, and other items (the teacher may make changes by adding new materials/items). The teacher will circulate to the groups and provide support and the teacher may question learners to highlight key math concepts. Each group will be given the opportunity to share with the large group/whole class the special foods, decorations and other items that are unique to the celebration.

DISCUSSIONHow are the celebrations similar or different?How are the decorations similar and different?Are the items the same or different from what your family uses?The teacher and learners complete this table during the discussion.| Celebration | Similar | Different || --

Learners give reasons for having the various persons in their group.
End of Week Three: Social Studies
Hosting a Family Celebration Culminating activity for the week

emphasis on the week's question: What are the special foods, decorations, events we experience in my family for these celebrations? Learners and parents collaborate to plan and host an event at school where families show and tell about a celebration in their house. Learners can be grouped for these activities; each group select a family celebration that they will showcase along with their parents. The school's population will be invited to view the display and sample some of the dishes exhibited. The kindergarten learners are expected to engage ‘visitors' to their booths in conversation sharing information about the foods, decorations and events of the celebrations.

Studies: Carnival VillagePrior to this activity, the learners will select the aspect of Carnival they would like to portray and parents will support by providing the necessary costume/materials they will need.Drama - Kiddies Carnival FrolicIMG:65:778:681

The school yard will be transformed into a Carnival village. Learners will parade in their costumes while being entertained by Carnival music (steel pan, calypso, soca, groovy). Parents and other stakeholders will assist with food stalls and managing the learners during the event. Popular soca and calypso artists will be invited to perform and interact with the learners.
Unit Wrap Up

Video Presentations and Playing CharadesReview the Essential Question of the Unit: What do I like about celebrations with my family?Have children look at a slide show depicting different celebrations (might be pictures of children and family or pictures taken during the duration of the unit). Children will select their favourite picture and talk about the celebration. Learners are encouraged to use correct terms to describe the type of celebration

religious or national.Encourage children to act out their experiences or what they have seen of a celebration/festival by using gestures, movements and expression but no words (charades). The other learners must guess what the celebration is (remind children to observe quietly so they can recognize the details). Learner then describe what he or she did.In small groups learners create a scrapbook about the various celebrations explored by drawing, using picture cutouts, and writing words or sentences.KWLThe teacher and learners will review what they put on the KWL chart earlier in this unit, and add information in the
